title: Case Law Pattern Intelligence
desc: Case law pattern intelligence derived from the Ailane data estate of 130,854+ classified UK employment tribunal decisions and 78,699+ employer profiles. This file provides the structured pattern data for the Knowledge Library's jurisprudential intelligence layer, governed by the Jurisprudential Intelligence System (JIS) defined in AILANE-SPEC-KLIA-001 Part IV. All tribunal-derived data is CREE-compliant (Claim Reporting and Employer Exposure Protocol) per ACEI Constitution Art. III §3.1. Individual employer identities are never disclosed in the KL — only anonymised aggregate patterns. Minimum threshold for pattern publication: ≥5 events and ≥3 distinct respondents. CREE contribution to any ACEI category score is capped at 15%.
disclaimerTier: Tier 2 (Standard) — Intelligence, not legal advice. Seek independent legal advice. Computation date noted.

patterns.title: Procedural Failure as Primary Ground
patterns.summary: Analysis of the Ailane tribunal decision estate shows that procedural failure remains the single most common ground for successful unfair dismissal claims. Employers who fail to follow the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures — particularly the requirements to investigate, notify in writing, hold a meeting, allow accompaniment, and offer an appeal — account for a disproportionate share of successful claims. The 25% uplift under TULRCA 1992 s.207A is applied in a significant proportion of cases where the Code is not followed.
keyFindings: Failure to investigate before disciplinary action is the most frequently cited procedural deficiency
Failure to allow the right to be accompanied is a common but easily avoidable error
Failure to offer a right of appeal is treated seriously even where the original decision was substantively fair
The Polkey reduction (reduction for chance of fair dismissal if procedure had been followed) is applied in a significant minority of successful claims — reducing but not eliminating the award
Small and medium employers are disproportionately represented in procedural failure claims — suggesting a training and awareness gap
keyAuthorities: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1988] ICR 142
BHS v Burchell [1980] ICR 303
Iceland Frozen Foods Ltd v Jones [1983] ICR 17
Taylor v OCS Group Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 702
patterns.dataNote: Pattern derived from CREE-compliant aggregate analysis. No individual employer identified. Minimum threshold: ≥5 events, ≥3 respondents.

patterns.title: Pregnancy and Maternity Discrimination — Rising Claim Volumes
patterns.summary: Pregnancy and maternity discrimination claims represent a growing proportion of the discrimination claim estate. Key patterns include dismissal during or shortly after maternity leave (often framed as redundancy but found to be pregnancy-related), failure to offer suitable alternative employment in redundancy situations where the employee is on maternity leave (breach of MPL 1999 reg.10), detriment for exercising maternity leave rights (including being excluded from promotion opportunities, training, or bonus schemes during maternity leave), and failure to carry out adequate risk assessments for pregnant employees under MHSWR 1999 reg.16.
keyFindings: Claims combining unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.99) and sex discrimination (EqA 2010 s.18) are common — the same facts often ground both claims
Failure to offer suitable alternative vacancy in redundancy is an absolute obligation — no balancing exercise required (Sefton v Wainwright [2015])
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 extended the protection period — employers must now check pregnancy/family leave status throughout the extended window
Awards are uncapped for discrimination claims — financial exposure is significantly higher than unfair dismissal alone
Injury to feelings awards have increased following the Vento guidelines update (Presidential Guidance 2025)
keyAuthorities: Brown v Rentokil Ltd [1998] ECJ Case C-394/96
Sefton Borough Council v Wainwright [2015] UKEAT/0168/14
Caledonia Bureau v Caffrey [1998] ICR 603
Atkins v Coyle Personnel plc [2008] IRLR 420
patterns.dataNote: Pattern derived from CREE-compliant aggregate analysis. No individual employer identified.

patterns.title: Protected Disclosure Detriment — Causation Challenges
patterns.summary: Whistleblowing claims present particular causation challenges. The claimant must prove that the protected disclosure was the reason or principal reason for the dismissal (s.103A) or the ground for the detriment (s.47B). The 'hidden reason' principle from Royal Mail v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55 allows tribunals to look behind the decision-maker's stated reason to the real reason operated on by the person who instigated the dismissal. Post-2013 claims must satisfy the public interest test from Chesterton v Nurmohamed [2017]. The absence of a cap on whistleblowing compensation means financial exposure is substantial.
keyFindings: Employers frequently fail to separate the response to the disclosure from the response to the discloser — the two must be treated independently
Settlement agreements cannot prevent workers from making protected disclosures (ERA 1996 s.43J) — NDAs attempting to prevent whistleblowing are void
The public interest test (post-2013) has reduced but not eliminated claims based on personal grievances
The 25% good faith reduction (ERA 1996 s.49(6A)) is rarely applied in practice — most successful claimants are found to have acted in good faith
Interim relief applications (ERA 1996 s.128) are increasing — successful applications require the employer to continue paying the employee pending full hearing
keyAuthorities: Royal Mail Group Ltd v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55
Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth [2018] EWCA Civ 1436
Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd [2008] EWCA Civ 380
NHS Manchester v Fecitt [2012] ICR 372
patterns.dataNote: Pattern derived from CREE-compliant aggregate analysis. No individual employer identified.

patterns.title: Service Provision Change — FM Sector Exposure
patterns.summary: The facilities management sector has the highest concentration of TUPE service provision change (SPC) claims in the Ailane data estate. FM employers face TUPE transfers on virtually every contract change — whether outsourcing, insourcing, or re-tendering. Key exposure areas include failure to provide employee liability information within 28 days (minimum £500 per employee penalty), failure to inform and consult affected employees (up to 13 weeks' pay per employee), and dismissals connected with the transfer without an ETO reason (automatically unfair). The 'organised grouping of employees' test is frequently litigated — employers must ensure they can identify which employees are assigned to which client contract.
keyFindings: Employee liability information failures are the most common TUPE compliance breach in FM — the 28-day notification requirement is frequently missed in fast-moving re-tender situations
Information and consultation failures often arise because the incoming contractor does not provide measures information to the outgoing contractor in time for meaningful consultation
Joint and several liability for consultation failures means the incoming contractor can inherit the outgoing contractor's failure to consult
Harmonisation of terms post-transfer remains unlawful unless there is an ETO reason entailing changes in the workforce (Berriman v Delabole Slate [1985])
Pension exclusion (reg.10) is narrow — enhanced redundancy benefits calculated by reference to pension provisions do transfer (Beckmann [2002])
keyAuthorities: Spijkers v Gebroeders Benedik [1986] ECJ Case 24/85
Enterprise Management Services Ltd v Connect-Up Ltd [2012] IRLR 190
Berriman v Delabole Slate Ltd [1985] ICR 546
Litster v Forth Dry Dock [1990] 1 AC 546
Manchester College v Hazel [2014] EWCA Civ 72
Beckmann v Dynamco Whicheloe Macfarlane Ltd [2002] ECJ Case C-164/00
patterns.dataNote: FM sector pattern analysis. CREE-compliant. No individual employer identified. Directly relevant to Northerly Hill demo entity.

patterns.title: National Minimum Wage Underpayment — Common Causes
patterns.summary: NMW underpayment claims and HMRC enforcement actions reveal consistent patterns of non-compliance. The most common causes are not deliberate underpayment but structural failures in payroll and working time calculation. Employers frequently fail to account for travel time between client sites (Whittlestone v BJP [2014]), fail to properly calculate NMW for salaried hours workers, fail to exclude the value of accommodation above the accommodation offset, and fail to account for time spent on mandatory training, security checks, or handover periods. The 200% financial penalty per worker (maximum £20,000 per worker) and the naming scheme create significant financial and reputational exposure.
keyFindings: Travel time between client sites during the working day counts as working time for NMW purposes
Mandatory training time, including online training, counts as working time
Uniform or equipment costs deducted from pay can reduce effective pay below NMW

Sleep-in shifts remain contentious — the Supreme Court in Royal Mencap Society v Tomlinson-Blake [2021] held that sleep-in workers are only entitled to NMW for time spent awake and working, not the entire shift
The HMRC naming scheme for NMW offenders creates reputational damage disproportionate to the financial penalty for many employers
keyAuthorities: Whittlestone v BJP Home Support Ltd [2014] ICR 275
Royal Mencap Society v Tomlinson-Blake [2021] UKSC 6
Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5
Leisure Employment Services Ltd v HMRC [2007] EWCA Civ 92
patterns.dataNote: Pattern derived from CREE-compliant aggregate analysis and published HMRC enforcement data. No individual employer identified.

patterns.title: Psychosocial Risk — Workplace Stress and Mental Health
patterns.summary: Workplace stress and mental health claims represent an increasing share of both the employment tribunal and personal injury claim estates. Employers face exposure through multiple legal routes: unfair dismissal for failure to manage work-related stress, personal injury claims for psychiatric injury caused by workplace stress (common law negligence), disability discrimination where stress-related conditions meet the EqA 2010 s.6 definition of disability, and HSE enforcement for failure to assess psychosocial risks under MHSWR 1999 reg.3. The HSE Management Standards for work-related stress provide the practical framework for compliance but are guidance rather than law.
keyFindings: Stress-related absence is the leading cause of long-term sickness absence in the UK workforce
Employers are expected to assess psychosocial risks as part of their general risk assessment obligation (Allison v LUL [2008])
The threshold for personal injury claims is foreseeability — the employer must have known or ought to have known that the employee was at risk (Hatton v Sutherland [2002])
Stress-related conditions may constitute a disability under EqA 2010 if they have a substantial and long-term adverse effect on day-to-day activities
HSE enforcement for psychosocial risk failures is increasing — improvement notices for stress management are now issued regularly
keyAuthorities: Hatton v Sutherland [2002] EWCA Civ 76
Barber v Somerset County Council [2004] UKHL 13
Allison v London Underground Ltd [2008] ICR 636
Walker v Northumberland County Council [1995] ICR 702
patterns.dataNote: Pattern analysis combining tribunal decision data, published HSE enforcement data, and personal injury case law. CREE-compliant where tribunal data is used.

surveillanceStatus.status: active — patterns refreshed on tribunal data estate update cycle
surveillanceStatus.note: Pattern intelligence is derived from the Ailane tribunal decision estate. Patterns are refreshed when new tribunal decisions are ingested and classified. The current 6 patterns cover the primary ACEI categories. Additional patterns will be added as the data estate grows and as ERA 2025 provisions commence — new claim types will generate new patterns.
pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 commencement will generate new pattern categories: zero hours claims, fire-and-rehire claims, day-one unfair dismissal claims
Worker Protection Act 2023 s.40A claims — monitoring for first tribunal outcomes
ERA 2025 trade union provisions — monitoring for industrial action dispute trends

constitutionalBasis.ACEI: ACEI Constitution Art. III §3.1 — Claim Reporting and Employer Exposure Protocol (CREE). All tribunal-derived data must comply with CREE anonymisation and minimum threshold requirements. CREE contribution capped at 15% of any ACEI category score.
constitutionalBasis.RRI: RRI Constitution Art. III §3.1 — Regulatory readiness assessment draws on tribunal outcome patterns to identify compliance risk areas.
constitutionalBasis.CCI: CCI Constitution Art. II §2.3 — Conduct assessment incorporates jurisprudential trends to identify systemic employer behaviour patterns.

dataGovernance.source: Ailane data estate — 130,854+ classified UK employment tribunal decisions and 78,699+ employer profiles
dataGovernance.anonymisation: Individual employer identities are never disclosed in the Knowledge Library. Only anonymised aggregate patterns published.
dataGovernance.minimumThreshold: ≥5 events and ≥3 distinct respondents required for pattern publication
dataGovernance.CREECap: CREE contribution to any ACEI category score capped at 15%
dataGovernance.clinicalNeutrality: Pattern intelligence is deterministic, not advisory. Findings state what the data shows — no recommendations on employer action. Consistent with Clinical Neutrality Standard (ACEI Constitution Art. I §1.4).

governingArchitecture.disclaimerTier: Tier 2 (Standard) — Intelligence, not legal advice. Seek independent legal advice. Computation date noted.
